one_line_takeaway: AdvPT shows that adversarial robustness of a frozen CLIP image encoder can be improved cheaply by tuning only text-side prompt vectors against a precomputed adversarial-embedding bank, complementing rather than replacing image-space denoising defenses.
key_finding: Tuning only text prompts materially raises adversarial robustness of a frozen image encoder. Under white-box PGD-40 (16/255), AdvPT beats vanilla hand-crafted-prompt CLIP by large margins on all 8 datasets and both backbones (e.g., +31.0 pts on Flowers and +33.2 on EuroSAT for ViT-B/16; +35.9 on Flowers and +38.9 on EuroSAT for ViT-L/14). Under AutoAttack, vanilla CLIP collapses to 0.0% everywhere while AdvPT recovers non-trivial accuracy (e.g., 23.3% Flowers, 28.9% DTD on ViT-B/16). AdvPT is orthogonal to input-denoising defenses and combines synergistically (a claimed '1+1>2' effect, e.g. ViT-B/16 Flowers: Super-resolution alone 13.8% -> +AdvPT 60.4%). It is at least ~100x more time-efficient than adversarial training and even beats Fast AT. A control against linear-probe CLIP (which also uses the extra downstream data) shows the robustness gain is NOT merely a consequence of additional data or higher clean accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Adversarial Prompt Tuning (AdvPT), a defense that improves the adversarial robustness of a frozen CLIP image encoder purely by tuning learnable TEXT prompt vectors, without any adversarial training, image-encoder modification, or architectural change. Its distinguishing idea (vs. cited work) is to invert where robustness is injected: rather than robustifying the image branch (adversarial training, visual prompt tuning) or denoising the input image, AdvPT precomputes adversarial image embeddings into a static 'adversarial embedding bank', discards the image encoder, then optimizes text-side learnable vectors so the clean text embeddings realign with the adversarial image embeddings. This is explicitly built on the CoOp prompt-learning framework (cited) but repurposes it from boosting clean accuracy to boosting adversarial robustness. The authors position it as complementary to (not a replacement for) input-denoising defenses and show a synergistic combination.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 3.2. The adversary has full white-box knowledge of the architecture and parameters of BOTH the image and text encoders and can perturb the image input under an l-infinity constraint ||delta||_inf <= epsilon. Crucially, the adversary has NO control over the textual input and NO knowledge of the prompt tuning; therefore text-modality adversarial attacks are considered out of scope. Attacks evaluated: white-box PGD-40 (maximizing KL divergence in embedding space) and AutoAttack (maximizing image-text contrastive loss), and black-box RAP (transfer attack using a ResNet-50 surrogate). The security goal is preserving zero-shot/downstream image-recognition accuracy under adversarial image perturbations.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) scope restricted to image-recognition/classification with contrastive CLIP-style VLMs; not extended to generative tasks like VQA or GPT-4V-style models. (2) Visual prompts, though promising for robustness, are excluded because they add branches and fall into the model-robustification category. Reviewer-noted: absolute robust accuracies remain modest under strong attacks (AutoAttack numbers are single-digit to ~29% for AdvPT alone; strong results depend on pairing with DiffPure). The threat model assumes the adversary is unaware of the prompt tuning, so no adaptive attack that targets the learned vectors is evaluated. A generalization-robustness trade-off is observed vs. CoOp (clean accuracy can drop), only partly mitigated at larger scale. Interpretability of the learned vectors is weak: nearest-word decoding (Table 5) yields non-intuitive tokens.

datasets_used: ImageNet (1,000 test images, one per class)
quantitative_results: PGD-40 (16/255), ViT-B/16 Flowers: vanilla CLIP 6.4% -> AdvPT 37.4% (+31.0)
AutoAttack, ViT-B/16: vanilla 0.0% on all 8 datasets; AdvPT alone 23.3% (Flowers), 28.9% (DTD), 11.0% (ImageNet)
Synergy PGD-40, ViT-B/16 Flowers: DiffPure 59.4% -> +AdvPT 81.9%; Rescale 60.1% -> +AdvPT 87.5%
Domain shift (Fig 6), ViT-B/16 ImageNet-R robust acc: vanilla 9.3% -> AdvPT 22.0%
Linear-probe CLIP control (Table 4), ViT-B/16 Flowers PGD-40: only 4.8% robust (far below AdvPT 37.4%), showing extra data alone does not confer robustness
Efficiency: AdvPT at least ~100x (order of magnitude) faster than PGD-10 adversarial training on Pets, and outperforms Fast AT
Hyperparameters: M=32 learnable vectors; train-time bank PGD-10 at 8/255; test-time PGD-40 at 16/255; RAP 400 iterations
baselines_compared: Vanilla CLIP with hand-crafted prompts (primary baseline)
CoOp (clean-accuracy prompt tuning) for generalization-robustness comparison
Linear-probe CLIP (control for extra downstream data)
Input-denoising defenses: DiffPure, Super-resolution, Rescale (randomization) — combined with AdvPT
Adversarial Training (PGD-10 AT) and Fast AT (efficiency comparison)
